channel.title: Los Angeles Review of Books – Reviews
channel.description: Book reviews from the Los Angeles Review of Books.

item.title: Love Transmuted into Archive
item.description: Netflix’s latest Istanbul melodrama adapts Orhan Pamuk’s 2008 novel into a multilayered exploration of class and gender tensions, memory curation, and tragic limerence.

item.title: The Narratives We Need
item.description: Samantha Mills’s stories hold a mirror up to our society and reveal all the ways it is deeply and fundamentally broken.

item.title: The Failure of Bildungsroman
item.description: In Indonesian author Eka Kurniawan’s new novel, crime and its punishment are the ultimate grammar of collectivity.

item.title: Story Is Haunting
item.description: Ali Smith’s new novel is less a story than a meditation on story.

item.title: Murky Effects
item.description: A novel of desire and domestic disorder amid a disintegrating literary establishment.

item.title: Spectacles of Repression: On Netflix’s “Beef”
item.description: Amanda Walujono reviews Netflix’s new dramedy “Beef.”

item.title: This Explains Everything
item.description: The Grateful Dead, professional wrestling, and the vertigo of the pop culture microhistory.

item.title: Aquatic Southern Gothic
item.description: Erin McCoy’s debut novel draws out the uncanny nature of environmental histories of the American South.

item.title: Maybe the World Will Muddle Through Somehow
item.description: AI overlord Demis Hassabis is the anti–Sam Altman. Does it matter?

item.title: Consummating Our Incomplete Nation
item.description: A newly translated Montenegrin novel combines dashes of Kafka, Kadare, and early Kundera with a style that is all Stefan Bošković’s own.

item.title: This Possible Grain of Light
item.description: Fanny Howe’s posthumous epic poem reads like an intercepted signal that explores what is ‘Unlocatable and Hidden.’

item.title: The Hedgehog and the Holocaust
item.description: Jochen Hellbeck’s recent history of the Eastern Front gets caught up in hyperfixating on its one big argument, at the cost of oversimplification.

item.title: Being Eugène Atget
item.description: The new exhibition of Eugène Atget’s photography demands what we are getting worse at giving: time, stillness, and the willingness to see.

item.title: Back to the Canadian Future
item.description: Two new music-minded Canadian comedies are fueled by nostalgia for the recent past.
